coredata.dc:title: Sulphur and nitrogen doped carbon dots synthesis by microwave assisted method as quantitative analytical nano-tool for mercury ion sensing
coredata.dc:creator: Pajewska-Szmyt, Martyna
Buszewski, Bogusław
Gadzała-Kopciuch, Renata
coredata.dc:description: Quantum dots prepared from carbon precursors are a rising star among novel trends in the field of carbon materials. The subject of this paper is the synthesis of carbon dots that can serve as a sensor for determination of heavy metals such as mercury. Carbon dots were obtained in this study with a fast method of microwave synthesis and application of citric acid with glutathione or thiourea. Both versions of carbon dots have optical properties, i.e. fluorescence; however, when thiourea was used as a doping element precursor, the dots made better sensors as – due to quenching by mercury ions – the carbon dots synthesized from citric acid and thiourea have more significant optical effect than those made form citric acid and glutathione. As-synthesized carbon dots had relative quantum yield (QY) as 26% and the limit of quantification when these dots were used was 5.4 μM. In order to test the sensor efficiency in real samples, river water and wastewater were used.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Mercury
Heavy metals
Sensor
Fluorescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence stability of S,N-CDs and quantum yield

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: IR spectra of CDs: CA and GSH (blue line), and CA + thiourea (red line).
Fluorescence spectra of CDs made from CA and thiourea.
Fluorescence intensity in absence (green) and presence (blue) of mercury. Left graph – sensor made from CA and GSH, right graph – from CA and thiourea.
TEM images of carbon dots made from CA and thiourea.
Influence of pH on carbon dot fluorescence and the quenching effect of mercury.
Influence of ionic strength on CD stability.
Influence of time on the fluorescence in absence (blue) and presence (red) of mercury.
A) Fluorescence response of the N,S-CDs to different metal ions; The selectivity of N,S-CDs toward Hg2+ in the absence and presence of other metal ions B) in ratio 1:1 and C)1:10.
Fluorescence emission spectra of N,S-CDs in the presence of increasing Hg2+ concentration (2.5–270 μM).
The linear relationship between F0/F and the Hg2+ in the range between 5.0 and 50 μM.
Examples of carbon dots reported to be used as a sensor for the mercury ion determination.
Elemental analysis of as-prepared carbon dots.
Parameters of the calibration curve for mercury (II) standard solution (buffer) and for contaminated real samples such as river water and wastewater.
Determination of Hg2+ in real samples such as river water and wastewater (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Sulphur and nitrogen doped carbon dots synthesis by microwave assisted method as quantitative analytical nano-tool for mercury ion sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Environmental Chemistry and Bioanalytics

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Quantum dots prepared from carbon precursors are a rising star among novel trends in the field of carbon materials. The subject of this paper is the synthesis of carbon dots that can serve as a sensor for determination of heavy metals such as mercury. Carbon dots were obtained in this study with a fast method of microwave synthesis and application of citric acid with glutathione or thiourea. Both versions of carbon dots have optical properties, i.e. fluorescence; however, when thiourea was used as a doping element precursor, the dots made better sensors as – due to quenching by mercury ions – the carbon dots synthesized from citric acid and thiourea have more significant optical effect than those made form citric acid and glutathione. As-synthesized carbon dots had relative quantum yield (QY) as 26% and the limit of quantification when these dots were used was 5.4 μM. In order to test the sensor efficiency in real samples, river water and wastewater were used.

ce:section.ce:para: In order to look for new analytical methods, efforts are being made to create new analytical tools that will ensure rapid and accurate determination. Nowadays, the “nano” prefix denotes new trends in science, and the concept of nanosensors is becoming increasingly popular [1]. Such nano-tools undoubtedly include carbon dots, which in recent years have been employed in the area of analytical chemistry, for example as selective sensors. Carbon dots (CDs) are promising materials which can be used as a tool in quantification methods. The properties of carbon dots are dependent on many factors, starting with the choice of the carbon source (precursor), and the synthesis path [2]. These nanotools have such properties as excellent solubility in water, good biocompatibility, chemical inertness, high resistance to photo bleaching, and high-stability structure; they can be easily functionalized while their toxicity is negligible [3,4]. However, this type of carbon material has also demonstrated such unique optical properties as luminescence [5]. For this reason, carbon dots can be regarded as a new generation of fluorescent nanomaterials which can enhance analytical performance. They can be used as a sensor for quantification analysis using the interaction between CDs and analytes (e.g. heavy metal) after calibration with a chemical standard. CDs can work in turn-off and turn-on mode. After interaction with the investigated analyte, photoluminescence of sensor is quenched (in most cases) or enhanced [3,6]. Citric acid is a typical reagent used in carbon dot synthesis as a result of its capacity to form carbonaceous materials through bottom-up thermal approach [7]. In order to enhance quantum efficiency and specificity of carbon dots, various methods of surface modification are used. One of the methods is heteroatom elemental doping, using the precursor elements such as nitrogen, sulphur or boron during the synthesis of carbon dots [8–10]. This is a common method to improve their photoluminescence properties by inserting atoms other than carbon, oxygen and hydrogen. A number of experiments have been carried out so far to obtain a selective sensor for mercury ions. Table 1 presents an illustrative example of the results of these experiments, including the preparation methods which are mostly used is microwave and hydrothermal synthesis [11,12]. However, two things must be noted regarding the limits of detection and the range of linearity in this set: the detection limit is often presented in a different unit than the linearity range, which in our opinion poses an obstacle to correct reception. In addition, the linearity range is given from point zero while from an analytical point of view it should not be lower than the limit of quantification.

Considering that carbon dots are a highly intriguing subject and that literature reports suggest that they can be used as a sensor for determination of mercury, pilot experiments have been undertaken to obtain such a sensor and to compare the results obtained with other methods – an important task considering that carbon dots can be a good alternative to such instrumental methods as atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS) or inductively coupled plasma-mass spectroscopy (ICP-MS), which are mostly used for metal detection. However, this technique requires first of all, expensive equipment and secondly, skilled operators, which limits its applicability [13]. Compared to this, fluorescence methods are easier to perform and provide quick response while maintaining high selectivity. Moreover, CDs have a number of benefits over other conventional fluorescent substances such as organic dyes, which are susceptible to photobleaching, while quantum dots form highly toxic metals. Furthermore, synthesis of carbon dots uses easily available and inexpensive carbon precursors and is easy to execute, while limiting the release of harmful by-products [14]. Thus the aim of the current study was to synthesize a sensor dedicated for mercury ion detection. Preparation of carbon dots is carried out by a microwave-assisted method. As mercury ions changed the native fluorescence of as-prepared carbon dots, a quenching mechanism was used. In addition, the method was also used for the analysis of environmental samples such as river water and wastewater. Validation parameters and recoveries were determined for both artificial samples in the buffer and environmental samples. The paper highlights the impact of the matrix which is rich in interfering compounds.

The materials and reagents used in this study included: anhydrous citric acid (Merck, Germany), l-glutathione (reduced), thiourea (Sigma-Aldrich, USA), sodium hydroxide (POCH, Gliwice), acetic acid (J.T. Baker), orthophosphoric acid and boric acid (POCH, Gliwice) for preparation of the Britton-Robinson buffer, quinine sulfate (suitable for fluorescence, Sigma-Aldrich, USA), potassium chloride (KCl), iron (III) chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O), calcium chloride (CaCl2), magnesium sulfate (MgSO4), copper (II) sulfate (CuSO4), manganese sulfate monohydrate (MnSO4
⋅
H2O), barium chloride dihydrate (BaCl2·2H2O), zinc chloride (ZnCl2), cadmium nitrate (Cd(NO3)2), mercury (II) chloride (HgCl2) (POCH, Gliwice). All solutions were prepared with deionized water from a Milipore MilliQ System.

The morphology and size of obtained carbon dots was characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) Tecnai F20 X-Twin whereas confirmation of functional group and doped elements was conducted by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) (Vertec 70 V) and elemental analysis (Vario EL Cube CHNS); optical properties were assessed by fluorescence spectroscopy (Jasco FP-8300, Europe).

1 g of citric acid and 1 g of l-glutathione or thiourea were dissolved in 10 ml deionized water. Then the solution was ultrasonicated for 10 min. The carbon dots were synthesized with the microwave-assisted method set for 5 min, 550 W. The obtained dark brown solid was dissolved in 20 ml water and stirred for 10 min. The solution was centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 15 min and filtered on a 0.22 μm membrane filter. Finally the solution was evaporated under nitrogen stream at room temperature until dry.

The solution of carbon dots was prepared by dissolving a proper amount of CDs in water. N,S-CDs were adjusted to the concentration of 191.45 μg/mL 200 μL of CD solution was added to 4 mL of universal buffer solution (pH = 7); then 200 μL Hg(II) solution with varied concentration was added. The fluorescence spectra (excited at 358 nm) were recorded after 25 min.

The selectivity of as-prepared carbon dots was studied by mixing N,S-CDs with each of the following ions: Ba2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, K+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Pb2+, and Zn2+, Ag+, NH4 +, Al3+, Cr3+; fluorescence intensities of dots were measured in the presence (F) and in the absence of (F0) interference. Furthermore, possible interference was evaluated in order to investigate cross reactivity. The mixture of Hg2+ and other metal ions have been prepared at the same concentration level and also with their content ten times as high as that of mercury. Fluorescence intensity was investigated after 25 min incubation.

To verify the potential use of the obtained carbon dots, real samples containing wastewater and river water were selected. Before analysis, the collected samples had been filtered to remove solids with a membrane filter (0.22 μm). A series of standard mercury solutions (0–37.5 μM) was prepared, and validation parameters were determined, taking into account the environmental matrix by mixing 4 ml of the matrix with 200 μL of the standard. Then 200 μL of CD solution was added. For recovery determination, real samples were contaminated with standard mercury solutions at 10.0, 12.5 and 15.0 μM for river water samples, and 15.0, 22.5, 37.5 μM for wastewater, and then mixed. Finally, the sensor was added to the prepared mixture. Subsequently, after a period of incubation the fluorescence spectra were collected. All measurements were taken in three repetitions (n = 3).

Quinine sulfate was used as a standard for determination of quantum yield of as-prepared carbon dots. The fluorescence spectra of Quinine sulfate (in 0.1 M H2SO4) and carbon dots solution as well as their absorbances were recorded at 360 nm. The following formula was used to calculate Quantum Yield.
[1]
Q
c
=
Q
s
×
F
c
⋅
A
s
⋅
n
c
2
F
s
⋅
A
c
⋅
n
s
2
where s is standard of quinine sulfate and c is carbon dot solution, F is integrated emission intensity, A is optical absorbance at 360 nm, and n is the refractive index of the solvent [20].

In this study N,S-CDs were prepared by a fast, microwave-assisted method. Anhydrous citric acid (CA) was used as the main carbon source. However, to improve fluorescence effect, a doping element such as nitrogen and sulphur was introduced.

Two types of synthesized carbon dots were characterized by elemental analysis in order to check the content of such elements as carbon, hydrogen, nitrogen and sulphur (Table 2 ). Glutathione (C10H17N3O6S) or thiourea (CH4N2S) was used to introduce doping elements to the surface of the dots (core doping) in order to enhance both their fluorescence and sensory properties. The elemental analysis shows that the content of carbon (C) was slightly higher for carbon dots produced with thiourea while hydrogen content is almost the same for both doping elements. However, nitrogen and sulphur content was significantly higher when thiourea was used for the synthesis, which suggests that the use of a molecule with a lower molar mass (thiourea 76 g/mol; GSH 307 g/mol) and a lower carbon/nitrogen/sulphur elementary ratio (thiourea 1/2/1 and GSH 10/3/1) was preferable in order to obtain the desired carbon dots. In a thiourea molecule, the content of nitrogen and sulphur elements is 50% and 25%, respectively, of the total molecule. On the other hand, GSH has relatively lower content of nitrogen (21%) and sulphur (7%).

To determine the functional groups on the surface of N,S-CDs, the IR spectra was investigated (Fig. 1 ). These results also confirmed that a more efficient synthesis was carried out using thiourea (red spectrum). The broad absorption bands of IR at 3200 cm−1 and 3028 cm−1 are characteristic peaks of O–H and N–H stretching vibrations. The presence of these functional groups provides excellent water solubility. The peaks at around 1650 cm−1 and 1403 cm−1 are in conformity with the vibrations of CN and C–N bonds, respectively. C–S bonds are noted by absorption peak at 1182 cm−1. Summarizing, the use of citric acid and thiourea in the synthesis of carbon dots led to efficient immobilization of amino and thiol groups.

As for the optical properties of N,S-CDs, Fig. 2 shows typical fluorescence spectra. N,S-CDs with GSH have maximum excitation and maximum emission at 350 nm and 422 nm respectively; on the other hand, CDs with thiourea display maximum excitation and emission wavelengths at 358 nm and 436 nm, respectively (Fig. 2). Based on these results, those wavelengths were selected as optimal for further analysis and verification of the detection properties. As a rapid test to the application of potential sensor for mercury determination, carbon dot solution (20 μg/mL) was prepared, and mercury solution was added to it to achieve a concentration of 0.15 mM. As shown in Fig. 3 , the sensitivity of the GSH sensor is low in relation to the analyte; much more promising results were obtained for the thiourea sensor, where the quenching caused by toxic metal is significant. Therefore further research was focused only on the characterization and optimization of carbon dots obtained from citric acid and thiourea by using microwave-assisted synthesis. The distinguishable effects of change in fluorescence are caused by non-radiative recombination. The creation of non-luminescent connection between N,S-CDs, which are the electron donor, and mercury (II) as electron acceptor is the cause of quenching of native fluorescence. Such analyte as Hg2+ may be associated with the ability to promote electron/hole recombination through electron transfer process [13,15,17].

The morphology of as-prepared carbon dots derived from citric acid and thiourea was characterized by TEM. The particle size was measured with the TEM image and ImageJ software (Fig. 4 ). The average diameter of carbon dots was 14.557 nm (SD = 2.429 nm). The obtained dots were characterized by a size larger than 10 nm, while other published studies obtained 2–6 nm dots most often (Table 1). This leads to considering the influence the synthesis method has on the size of the dots obtained. However, it is also quite possible that particles aggregated, which makes it difficult to determine their exact size and shape precisely.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence stability of S,N-CDs and quantum yield
ce:section.ce:para: The influence of pH, time and ionic strength on the optical properties of as-synthesized N,S-CDs was investigated.

The Britton-Robinson buffer (pH 2–12) at different pH was used as medium for investigating the behavior of carbon dots. Relative fluorescence intensity was studied in the absence (F0) and presence (F) of mercury ions in the solution. At acidic and basic pH the fluorescence of carbon dots decreased. Moreover, the quenching effect caused by mercury also changed with different pH. At lower pH (2–4), nitrogen and sulphur elements repulsed mercury ions as a consequence of protonation of these atoms and the quenching effect was unobservable [15]. The optimal medium was the Britton-Robinson buffer at pH = 7 as it offered the highest fluorescence and quenching effect (Fig. 5 ). This medium was thus chosen for further investigation.
An significant factor which characterizes the obtained fluorescent material is the quantum yield (QY), which in the case of carbon dots derived from thiourea and citric acid is 26%.

In order to evaluate the suitability of carbon dots for potential applications, the influence of other ions on fluorescence intensity was evaluated. The selectivity of the N,S-CDs for the detection of Hg(II) was assessed and other metal ions were added into the sensor medium. The F0–F calculation was used so as to estimate the detection system of N,S-CDs. Fig. 8 shows the quenching effect caused by various metal ions, including such analytes as Hg2+ and other metals like Ba2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, K+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Ag+, NH4 +, Al3+, Cr3+. The largest quenching was caused by mercury (II) ions, which was expected and desirable. It could be seen that except iron (III) and silver(I), other metals did not significantly affect the fluorescence intensity (Fig. 8A). Moreover, the presence of interfering metals in the mixture with mercury rarely causes changes in FL intensity. These data show that as-prepared carbon dots are sensitive to mercury (II) in the presence of other ions (Fig. 8B and C). An explanation of this effect can be faster coordination of Hg2+ to the functional groups on the surface of carbon dots and strong binding with doped elements such as S and N [16]. As a result, the carbon dots obtained from citric acid and thiourea are highly selective and have a potential application as a sensor for determination of mercury.

A series of standard solutions of mercury (2.5 μM–270 μM) has been prepared to determine the linearity and the limit of detection (LOD) and quantification (LOQ) of the method using the obtained sensor (Fig. 9 ). Following the procedure described in 2.4, a series of quenching was conducted.

The calibration curve was obtained by plotting the intensity of fluorescence (F0/F) versus different concentration of mercury ions; as the data shows, linearity was achieved between 5 μM and 50 μM of mercury ions (Fig. 10 ). Limits of detection and quantification were estimated based on the standard deviation of the response (δ) of the curve and the slope of the calibration curve (S). The LOD was calculated according to the equation: LOD = 3.3·δ/S and LOQ = 10 δ/S. The standard deviation of the response was designated based on the standard deviation of y-intercepts of regression lines. Calculations showed that the limit of detection was 1.78 μM and limit of quantification is 5.4 μM. Unfortunately, these results are not satisfactory as too high LOQ complicates the potential application of the sensor for real samples such as biological materials because such high concentrations are not expected in samples. The use of this sensor would require the use of a large sample volume and long sample concentration, which is not desirable for biological samples and due to the duration of the analytical procedure. However, these results are a good basis for further consideration and encourage further work on this type of sensor.

As is presented in Table 1, the obtained carbon dots are characterized by LOD at 1.78 μM level, which is similar to the results of Liu and co-workers [10]. However, in this study LOD is much higher than in other works. However, what should be noted is the range of linearity of the sensor described by the research teams. This information is very general and, in our opinion, incorrect when considering the validation of a method: for example, in the works of Tabaraki et al. (2018) [16], Ma et al. (2016) [17] or Iqbal et al. (2018) [9] linearity range begins at 0 point. According to the validation rules, the calibration curve should not start below the LOQ point. Therefore, the use of a calibration curve that starts at point 0 is not compatible with these criteria, and gives a misleading view of the potential use of the sensor in the analytical method. As-synthesized carbon dots have quantum yield of 26%; this result is not significantly different from the other examples included in Table 1. However, the carbon dots obtained by microwave assisted synthesis are relatively larger than those coming from other hydrothermal syntheses. In our work their diameter was 14.5 nm; slightly smaller CDs were obtained in Tabaraki and Sadeghinejad's [16] study (10 nm). The examples of hydrothermal syntheses presented in Table 1 show that it is possible to obtain particles below 3.5 nm in size.

To evaluate the practical application of the proposed sensor, river water and wastewater were investigated. According to the procedure described in section 2.4 and 2.6, the validation parameters and recovery were investigated. The results including the LOD, LOQ and parameters of calibration curve are presented in Table 3 . The river sample was obtained from the Vistula and spiked with mercury (II) solution. The resulting regression coefficient for river calibration curve is slightly lower (R2 = 0.9995) compared to the buffer sample (R2 = 0.9996). However, significant differences can already be bridged for the LOD, which is 3.23 μM, which is almost double the value obtained for standards in buffer solution. What can be observed here is a so-called matrix effect, which can be caused by such interferences as mineral compounds and other impurities in real water. In case of wastewater, i.e. a sample of a much more heavily loaded matrix, the detection limit has increased significantly to 9.81 μM and the regression coefficient decreased (R2 = 0.9949). The recoveries determined for the actual samples at three concentration levels are shown in Table 4 . As can be seen, the recoveries for the river sample are at a highly satisfactory level (86.1–115.1%). However, once again, in the case of wastewater, a significant influence of interfering substances is observed. The consequence of this is a much lower recovery rate (42.5–48.1%). However, attention should be drawn to the very high repeatability in both cases (SD < 2.3). Summarizing the application of the obtained sensor to real samples, it is necessary to pay particular attention to the influence of the matrix, which significantly affects the validation parameters of the method. However, high repeatability of results is highly positive and motivates to further work on methods utilizing this type of sensor, which is particularly interesting considering the ease and low cost of carbon dot synthesis.

To sum up, microwave synthesis method is a simple, fast and cheap method for preparation of carbon dots. Both types of N,S-carbon dots obtained in the study display fluorescence properties. However, when citric acid was used as the main carbon source and thiourea as the doping element, it was possible to obtain a sensor quite well suited for mercury determination. However, if the resulting sensor is to be used for real samples, further research is needed to reduce the limit of quantification of the method. Nevertheless, developing an effective method for determination and quantification of mercury is of particular importance and further work in this field may contribute to producing rapid metal tests for biological samples. Although the sensitivity of the sensor is not comparable to other examples in literature, these studies show the simplicity, efficiency and rapidity of this potential way to determine the heavy metal. First of all, the ease of carbon dot synthesis and its low toxicity as well as harmlessness of the obtained sensor to the environment should be emphasized. Both the synthesis and the sensor are embedded in the Green Chemistry principles and fulfill the criteria of eco-friendly chemistry, which is highly desirable nowadays. They provide a solid basis for further work on improving, the sensor so that this kind of material can be used also in multidisciplinary research area.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was financially supported by the National Science Centre within the framework of the project OPUS No. 2018/29/B/ST4/01681.

(2019)588-596. doi:10.1016/j.saa.2018.07.097.
